policy_statement: E1. Retirement and/or Repurposing of Fossil Fuel Fired Facilities (strategy framework).
mechanism_description: The strategy plans for retiring and/or repurposing fossil fuel–fired facilities, supported by emissions targets, emissions regulations, and resource planning to reduce emissions from electricity generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Electricity generation shift (retire fossil plants) → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ power-sector GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities/system operators and policymakers accelerate the retirement, mothballing, fuel-switching, or repurposing (e.g., to synchronous condensers, storage sites, peaker replacements with clean resources) of coal/oil/gas-fired generating units through planning and regulatory actions.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels lowers direct stack emissions (primarily CO₂; also some CH₄/N₂O depending on fuel and operations) and shifts dispatch/capacity to lower-emitting resources (renewables, storage, demand response, efficiency, or cleaner firm capacity).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in grid emissions intensity and total electricity-sector GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced power-sector GHG emissions by eliminating or displacing fossil fuel combustion from electricity generation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Fossil plant retirement/repurposing reduces fossil generation and thus combustion emissions on the grid, producing direct GHG reductions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The primary intent and largest effect is lowering emissions (not primarily improving energy productivity, managing climate hazards, or enhancing ecosystems).  
3) Edge cases applied: If repurposing were mainly for resilience (e.g., converting sites to emergency backup) or for efficiency gains without major emissions change, classification could shift; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly targets grid decarbonization via fossil retirement, keeping Mitigation dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that fits this mechanism (planned retirement/repurposing of fossil-fired facilities to decarbonize the grid) has its main climate effect through reduced fossil-fuel electricity emissions.  
5) Note on subtypes: Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., coal vs gas, regulatory vs planning instrument, equity/just transition features) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Repurposing can sometimes retain fossil combustion (e.g., conversion to gas) or create resilience-focused backup generation; those cases may dilute mitigation impact, but the stated mechanism is explicitly fossil retirement/repurposing in service of grid decarbonization, so Mitigation remains primary.
key_indicators: "Retirement and/or Repurposing of Fossil Fuel Fired Facilities"; "emissions reduction targets"; "Promulgation of emissions regulations"; "Regular and transparent resource planning"
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements (reduced co-pollutants), potential grid reliability modernization via repurposing—mentioned implicitly via repurposing/planning but not explicit enough to classify as a separate category.
instance_edge_case_notes: The components list includes "emissions reduction targets" (could suggest target-setting) and "emissions regulations" (could suggest regulatory-mandate/performance-standard), but the excerpt is framed as a high-level "strategy" with planning components and no explicit binding threshold, schedule, or compliance details; thus classified as planning-strategy.
